metadata.title: Handle It Gracefully: Errors That Don't Crash the Workflow
metadata.playlist: Make It Smart — Scale Your Madison Intelligence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 4 brief — Scale Your Thing & Add Intelligence

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 2 of 7. The AI node you added in Episode 1 will fail. Not maybe — it will timeout, return malformed output, or hit a rate limit at some point. A workflow with no error handling dies on that first bad response and takes everything with it. A workflow with error handling logs the failure, keeps running on the other records, and gives you something to debug. No error handling costs ten points off the assignment — the rubric is explicit about it. Today you learn three n8n patterns for handling errors and fill the error-handling doc table. Let's go.

props.segment: Handle It Gracefully: Errors That Don't Crash the Workflow
props.command: Add error handling so the AI node fails gracefully instead of crashing the workflow.
props.runningText: no error handling = -10 pts; a crash on one bad record = all records lost…
output: 3 n8n patterns: error workflow · On-Error branch · Function try/catch
Retry with exponential backoff for rate limits and timeouts
Fill the error-handling doc table: what fails / how we handle it

beats.narration_text: n8n gives you three ways to handle errors. First: an Error Workflow. In n8n settings, you can define a separate workflow that runs whenever any other workflow fails — it receives the error details and can log them, send a notification, or store the failed record for manual review. This is the system-level catch. Second: On-Error branches on individual nodes. Click a node, open its settings, and look for an 'On Error' or error output option. When that node fails, instead of stopping the workflow, n8n routes the execution down the error branch — where you log the record that failed and pass an empty or fallback value to the next node. The rest of the records keep running. Third: try and catch inside a Function node. If your logic is in a JavaScript Function node, wrap the risky section in a try block with a catch block that logs the error and returns a safe default. These three patterns are not mutually exclusive — a production workflow often uses all three. For Assignment 4, the On-Error branch on the AI node is the minimum requirement. Check n8n.io/docs for the current node settings in your version.

artifactLines: 1. Error Workflow (system level): a separate workflow that catches any workflow failure — logs/notifies.
2. On-Error branch (node level): node fails → routes to error branch → log + continue on next record.
3. try/catch in Function node: wrap risky JS in try/catch → catch returns a safe default.
Minimum for A4: On-Error branch on the AI node.
Check n8n.io/docs for the current option name in your version — settings UI changes.
props.sparkLine: On-Error branch on the AI node. Minimum. Start there.

beats.act: retry and backoff for AI rate limits
beats.narration_text: AI APIs enforce rate limits. If you send fifty requests in ten seconds and the limit is ten per second, you will get rate-limit errors on the excess. The right response is not to give up — it is to retry with exponential backoff. Exponential backoff means: first retry after two seconds, second retry after four seconds, third after eight. Each retry waits twice as long as the previous one. This prevents your retries from immediately triggering another rate-limit error. In n8n, you can implement backoff in a Function node that uses the JavaScript wait pattern, or in some versions, n8n's built-in retry settings on a node. For timeouts — when the AI API takes too long to respond — set a maximum wait time and log the record as failed rather than blocking the entire workflow. Document both cases in the error-handling table: what error can occur, what the workflow does, and what the output looks like when the error happens.

props.artifactHeading: Rate limits and timeouts — how to handle them
artifactLines: Rate limit: too many requests too fast → retry with exponential backoff.
Backoff: retry after 2s → 4s → 8s. Each retry waits twice as long.
Why: retrying immediately just triggers another rate-limit error.
Timeout: AI takes too long → set max wait → log 'AI timeout' → continue on next record.
Document both: what error / what workflow does / what output looks like on failure.
props.sparkLine: Rate limit: back off. Timeout: log and continue.

beats.narration_text: The assignment's documentation section requires an error-handling table. Four columns: what could go wrong, how the workflow handles it, what the user or operator sees when it happens, and what manual action if any is required. Rows cover the failures you built handling for. AI API rate limit: handled by exponential backoff retry — operator sees nothing for the first three retries, sees a logged warning after the third failure. AI API timeout: handled by On-Error branch — the failed record is written to a failed-records log with a timestamp; no manual action required unless the log accumulates. Missing or malformed AI response: handled by On-Error branch — the record is written with error equals AI-malformed-response as the output field; requires manual review. Network failure: handled by the n8n error workflow — operator is notified; run paused for manual restart. Fill this table with the actual error types your AI node can produce. A table with two rows and no specific error types tells the grader you did not test the error cases.

props.artifactHeading: Error table — four columns, real failure modes
artifactLines: Columns: What fails · How handled · What user/operator sees · Manual action required?
Row: AI rate limit → exponential backoff retry → silent on first 3 retries, then log → none.
Row: AI timeout → On-Error branch → record written to failed-records log → none unless log grows.
Row: Malformed AI response → On-Error branch → record written with error= tag → manual review.
Row: Network failure → Error Workflow → operator notified → manual restart.
Specific errors = evidence you tested. Generic rows = evidence you didn't.
props.sparkLine: Specific failure modes. Real handling. Evidence of testing.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Handle It Gracefully. No error handling costs ten points — the rubric says so. The minimum for Part 1 is an On-Error branch on the AI node: when the node fails, log the error and continue on the next record. Three n8n patterns in order from easiest to most complete: the On-Error branch on the node, an Error Workflow at the system level, and try-catch logic inside a Function node. Add retry with exponential backoff for rate limits. Set a max timeout for slow AI responses. Fill the error-handling doc table with four columns and real failure modes — not generic rows that could apply to any workflow. A generic table signals that you did not actually test the error cases. Episode 3 builds the output that a human can actually use.

props.artifactTitle: Handle It Gracefully: Errors That Don't Crash the Workflow
artifactLines: No error handling = -10 pts. Minimum: On-Error branch on the AI node.
Three patterns: On-Error branch · Error Workflow · try/catch in Function node.
Rate limits: exponential backoff (2s → 4s → 8s). Timeouts: log and continue.
Doc table: 4 columns · real failure modes · not generic rows.
Generic error table = did not test. Specific failure modes = tested.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude to generate the error-handling table for your specific AI node. The prompt reads: I have an n8n workflow with an AI node that does this: [describe your AI task from Episode 1 in one sentence]. The AI service is [name of the service — for example OpenAI, Anthropic, or NewsAPI]. List the five most likely failure modes for this specific AI node — rate limits, timeouts, malformed responses, missing fields, and any service-specific errors — and for each: describe exactly what the error looks like in n8n, what the workflow should do when it happens, and whether the failed record needs manual review or can be automatically skipped. Then write the On-Error branch logic as a pseudocode block I can use to implement it in a n8n Function node. That gives you the table rows and the implementation pseudocode in one pass. Take the pseudocode into n8n and build the error branch. Then trigger the error case deliberately — disconnect from the internet, pass malformed input, or exceed the rate limit — and verify that the error branch fires correctly before you consider error handling done.

props.runningText: trigger the error case deliberately to verify the branch fires before calling it done…

beats.narration_text: Handle It Gracefully: Errors That Don't Crash the Workflow. Liam, in for Bear.
